subject: mathematics
title: Application of Integrals
narrative: This NCERT chapter is short and largely worked through examples; the discrete examinable concepts are few. The node set below covers every concept that has a clean verbatim prose statement in the source. Sub-skills that appear only inside worked computations (e.g. the closed-form integral of sqrt(a^2 - x^2)) are taught within the relevant area nodes rather than split out, to avoid fabricating quotes.

nodes.title: Form the elementary area of a thin strip
nodes.brief: Treat the area under a curve as built from thin vertical strips of area dA = y dx.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Slicing the region into vertical strips.
To find the area enclosed by a curve y = f(x), the x-axis, and the vertical lines x = a and x = b, picture the region as built from a large number of very thin vertical strips. Each strip has height equal to the curve value y = f(x) at that x and a tiny width dx.

PART 2 — Writing the elementary area dA = y dx.
The area of one such strip is dA = y dx, where y = f(x). This single expression is called the elementary area, and it is the building block for every area calculation in the chapter. Example: for y = x^2 between x = 0 and x = 1, a strip at position x has dA = x^2 dx.

PART 3 — Summing strips by integration.
Adding up all the elementary strips across the region from x = a to x = b gives the total area A = integral from a to b of y dx. Integration is precisely the continuous limit of this summation process.
keyMoves: Slice the region into thin vertical strips of width dx
Write the elementary area of a strip as dA = y dx with y = f(x)
Prepare to sum the strips by integration
misconceptions: Forgetting that the strip height is the curve value y = f(x)
Using a finite width instead of the infinitesimal dx

nodes.title: Find the area under a curve with respect to the x-axis
nodes.brief: Compute area bounded by y = f(x), the x-axis and x = a, x = b as the integral of y dx from a to b.
nodes.quote: the result of adding up the elementary areas of thin strips across the region
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Setting up the integral.
The total area A of the region between the curve y = f(x), the x-axis, and the ordinates x = a and x = b is found by integrating the elementary strip areas: A = integral from a to b of y dx = integral from a to b of f(x) dx. You just substitute the curve equation for y and integrate between the two x-limits.

PART 2 — Worked example: area under y = x from x = 0 to x = 2.
Here f(x) = x, so A = integral from 0 to 2 of x dx = [x^2/2] from 0 to 2 = (4/2) - 0 = 2. This matches the triangle with base 2 and height 2, which has area (1/2)(2)(2) = 2, confirming the formula.

PART 3 — Key check.
Always confirm that the curve lies above the x-axis (f(x) >= 0) on [a, b] before applying this formula directly; if the curve dips below, the integral is negative and you must handle the sign separately.
keyMoves: Express y = f(x) for the curve
Integrate y dx from x = a to x = b
Report A = integral from a to b of f(x) dx
misconceptions: Integrating with respect to the wrong variable
Using the wrong limits a and b for the region

nodes.title: Find the area with respect to the y-axis
nodes.brief: Compute area bounded by x = g(y), the y-axis and y = c, y = d as the integral of x dy from c to d.
nodes.quote: The area A of the region bounded by the curve x = g(y), y-axis and the lines y = c, y = d is given by
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Switching to horizontal strips.
When the boundary is more naturally written as x = g(y), use horizontal strips of width dy instead of vertical strips. Each strip has length x = g(y) and width dy, so its elementary area is dA = x dy = g(y) dy.

PART 2 — The formula.
The total area of the region bounded by x = g(y), the y-axis, and the lines y = c and y = d is A = integral from c to d of x dy = integral from c to d of g(y) dy. This is the mirror image of the x-axis case with the roles of x and y swapped.

PART 3 — Worked example: area bounded by x = y^2, y-axis, y = 0, y = 2.
Here g(y) = y^2, so A = integral from 0 to 2 of y^2 dy = [y^3/3] from 0 to 2 = 8/3 - 0 = 8/3. Horizontal strips give this directly; vertical strips would require inverting the curve and splitting the region.
keyMoves: Write the curve as x = g(y)
Use horizontal strips of width dy
Integrate x dy from y = c to y = d
misconceptions: Integrating y dx when horizontal strips (x dy) are intended
Mixing the x-limits with the y-limits

nodes.title: Take the numerical value of area below the x-axis
nodes.brief: When the curve dips below the x-axis the integral is negative; take its numerical (absolute) value as the area.
nodes.quote: it is only the numerical value of the area which is taken into consideration.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Why the integral is negative below the axis.
If the curve y = f(x) lies below the x-axis on [a, b], then f(x) < 0 everywhere on that interval. Each elementary strip area dA = f(x) dx is therefore negative, and the definite integral integral from a to b of f(x) dx comes out as a negative number.

PART 2 — Taking the numerical (absolute) value.
Since area is always a positive quantity, it is only the numerical value of the area which is taken into consideration. You take the absolute value: area = |integral from a to b of f(x) dx|. Example: for y = x - 2 on [0, 2], the curve is below the x-axis; the integral = [x^2/2 - 2x] from 0 to 2 = (2 - 4) - 0 = -2, so the area = |-2| = 2.

PART 3 — Practical check.
Before computing, sketch or test a point to confirm whether the curve is above or below the axis. A negative integral result is a signal to apply the absolute value, not an error in the calculation.
keyMoves: Detect where the curve lies below the x-axis (f(x) < 0)
Compute the (negative) integral there
Take its absolute / numerical value as the area
misconceptions: Reporting a negative number as the area
Assuming the integral always equals the area without checking the sign

nodes.title: Combine areas above and below the x-axis
nodes.brief: When a curve is partly above and partly below the axis, add the absolute pieces: A = |A1| + A2.
nodes.quote: the area A bounded by the curve y = f(x), x-axis and the ordinates x = a and x = b is given by  A = |A1| + A2 .
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Splitting at the crossing point.
When a curve y = f(x) crosses the x-axis at some point x = c between a and b, the curve is below the axis on [a, c] giving a negative integral A1, and above the axis on [c, b] giving a positive integral A2. You must split the interval at c and integrate each piece separately; integrating straight across lets the pieces cancel.

PART 2 — The formula A = |A1| + A2.
The total bounded area is A = |A1| + A2, where A1 = integral from a to c of f(x) dx (negative) and A2 = integral from c to b of f(x) dx (positive). Taking |A1| converts the negative piece to a positive area before adding.

PART 3 — Worked example: y = x - 1 from x = 0 to x = 2 (crosses axis at x = 1).
A1 = integral from 0 to 1 of (x - 1) dx = [x^2/2 - x] from 0 to 1 = (1/2 - 1) - 0 = -1/2. A2 = integral from 1 to 2 of (x - 1) dx = [x^2/2 - x] from 1 to 2 = (2 - 2) - (1/2 - 1) = 0 - (-1/2) = 1/2. Total A = |-1/2| + 1/2 = 1/2 + 1/2 = 1.
keyMoves: Find where the curve crosses the x-axis and split the interval there
Integrate over each sub-interval separately
Add the absolute values: A = |A1| + A2
misconceptions: Integrating straight across so positive and negative parts cancel
Forgetting to take the absolute value of the below-axis piece

nodes.title: Find the area enclosed by a circle using integration
nodes.brief: Use symmetry and integration of sqrt(a^2 - x^2) to recover the circle's area pi a^2.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Setting up using symmetry.
For the circle x^2 + y^2 = a^2 (centred at the origin, radius a), the upper half satisfies y = sqrt(a^2 - x^2) and the lower half y = -sqrt(a^2 - x^2). By the four-fold symmetry of the circle, the total area equals 4 times the first-quadrant area (where y >= 0 and x from 0 to a).

PART 2 — Evaluating the integral.
A = 4 times integral from 0 to a of sqrt(a^2 - x^2) dx. Using the standard result integral of sqrt(a^2 - x^2) dx = (x/2)sqrt(a^2 - x^2) + (a^2/2) arcsin(x/a) + C, evaluated from 0 to a: at x = a the expression gives (a^2/2)(pi/2) = pi*a^2/4; at x = 0 the expression gives 0. So the first-quadrant area is pi*a^2/4.

PART 3 — Final result.
A = 4 times (pi*a^2/4) = pi*a^2. This confirms the familiar formula using integration. For a = 3: area = pi(9) = 9*pi.
keyMoves: Solve the circle equation for y = sqrt(a^2 - x^2) in the first quadrant
Use symmetry: total area = 4 times the first-quadrant area
Integrate to obtain A = pi a^2
misconceptions: Keeping the negative root for y in the first quadrant

nodes.title: Find the area enclosed by an ellipse using integration
nodes.brief: Use symmetry and integration to get the area pi a b enclosed by an ellipse.
nodes.quote: Find the area enclosed by the ellipse
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Expressing y from the ellipse equation.
For the ellipse x^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 = 1 (semi-axes a along x, b along y), solving gives y = (b/a)sqrt(a^2 - x^2) in the upper half. The ellipse has the same four-fold symmetry as the circle, so the total area = 4 times the first-quadrant area.

PART 2 — Evaluating the integral.
A = 4 times integral from 0 to a of (b/a)sqrt(a^2 - x^2) dx = (4b/a) times integral from 0 to a of sqrt(a^2 - x^2) dx. From the circle calculation, integral from 0 to a of sqrt(a^2 - x^2) dx = pi*a^2/4. Therefore A = (4b/a)(pi*a^2/4) = pi*a*b.

PART 3 — Interpreting the result.
The area of the ellipse is pi*a*b. When a = b the ellipse becomes a circle and the formula correctly reduces to pi*a^2. For an ellipse with semi-axes a = 5 and b = 3: area = pi(5)(3) = 15*pi.
keyMoves: Solve the ellipse equation for y in the first quadrant
Use symmetry: total area = 4 times the first-quadrant area
Integrate to get A = pi a b
misconceptions: Dropping the factor b/a from the ellipse's y

nodes.title: Find the area of a region bounded by curves and lines
nodes.brief: Set up and evaluate the definite integral(s) for the area of a region bounded by given curves, axes and lines.
nodes.quote: the area under simple curves, area between lines and arcs of circles, parabolas and ellipses
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The general strategy.
To find the area of any region bounded by curves and/or lines: (1) sketch the region and identify every boundary; (2) find the intersection or crossing points that will serve as limits of integration; (3) decide whether vertical strips (integrate y dx along the x-axis) or horizontal strips (integrate x dy along the y-axis) are simpler.

PART 2 — Handling sign and splitting.
Split the interval at every point where a curve crosses the x-axis or where two bounding curves swap their relative position. Integrate each piece separately and add their absolute values so that no area cancels. For the area between two curves y = f(x) (top) and y = g(x) (bottom), with f(x) >= g(x) on [a, b], the area = integral from a to b of (f(x) - g(x)) dx.

PART 3 — Worked example: area between y = x^2 and y = x from x = 0 to x = 1.
Intersection: x^2 = x gives x = 0 and x = 1. On [0, 1], x >= x^2 (top curve is y = x). Area = integral from 0 to 1 of (x - x^2) dx = [x^2/2 - x^3/3] from 0 to 1 = (1/2 - 1/3) - 0 = 3/6 - 2/6 = 1/6.
keyMoves: Sketch the region and find intersection / crossing points for the limits
Choose vertical (y dx) or horizontal (x dy) strips
Integrate over each piece and add, using absolute values for below-axis parts
misconceptions: Integrating without sketching, so the limits or sign are wrong
Letting above- and below-axis parts cancel instead of adding their magnitudes
